python-internals.title: Global interpreter lock - Wikipedia
python-internals.description: A global interpreter lock (GIL) is a mutual-exclusion lock held by a programming language interpreter thread to avoid sharing code that is not thread-safe with other threads. In implementations with a GIL, there is always one GIL for each interpreter process.

python-internals.title: GlobalInterpreterLock
python-internals.description: In CPython, the global interpreter lock, or GIL, is a mutex that protects access to Python objects, preventing multiple threads from executing Python bytecodes at once. The GIL prevents race conditions and ensures thread safety.

python-internals.title: What Is the Python Global Interpreter Lock (GIL)? – Real Python
python-internals.description: Python's Global Interpreter Lock or GIL, in simple words, is a mutex (or a lock) that allows only one thread to hold the control of the Python interpreter at any one time. In this article you'll learn how the GIL affects the performance of your Python programs.

python-internals.title: Inside Python: How your code runs, from source to execution | Medium
python-internals.description: Your code goes through parsing, compiling, bytecode generation, executing inside a virtual machine, interacting with memory managers, and respecting the GIL. This entire process decides how fast your code runs, how much memory it uses, and even why some optimizations matter.
